title: Workbench availability SLA missing from core requirements; meta-requirement gap
reasoning: Milarna's analysis flags critical absence: 'The system (Workbench) that manages BVLOS authorization cases is not covered by requirements. System risks are: [bugs that corrupt case data], [false positive/negative completeness checks], [audit trail tampering], [unauthorized access]. Yet no requirement addresses: testing strategy for the system, version control, change management, backup/disaster recovery, or post-deployment monitoring.' No SLA defined for workbench availability, yet it directly impacts CAA case throughput (REQ001-REQ016 all depend on it).
recommendation: Add meta-requirement NFR-W001: 'As a CAA IT manager I want the Workbench system to be assured so that case data integrity and decision defensibility are maintained.' Specify: 99.5% business-hours availability for applicant intake, 99.9% for panel operations, 99.99% for regulatory archive, 100% for audit log writes. Include: RTO/RPO per tier, backup/disaster recovery plan (3-2-1 rule), version control for all configuration/rule changes, pre-deployment testing per CAA IT standard, post-deployment monitoring for correctness/performance/security.

title: Classification boundary service is a single point of failure with hard isolation dependency
reasoning: Classification boundary (CAP 722H / JSP 440 compliance) sits at intersection of unclassified CAA panel tooling and classified MOD evidence. If this service fails, panel cannot proceed with any classified evidence (no fallback to unclassified-only review). Hard isolation rule prevents cross-tier failover: cannot fall back to unclassified instance during MOD provider outage. Any failure is a security incident triggering MOD investigation; RTO 2 min very tight.
recommendation: Implement dual active instances (MOD-hosted + CAA-hosted) with independent redundancy. Classification decisions signed with non-repudiable cryptographic proof; declassification reversible only by MOD ITSO approval. Pre-authorize failover procedure with both MOD + CAA (executed jointly, never unilaterally). Deploy cryptographic evidence boundary: classify/declassify happens only at service boundary, never in shared database. Real-time alerting (SMS/Slack) on any classification error; escalates to security team <30 sec.

title: Audit deliberation log cannot tolerate any write loss; 100% availability mandatory but no redundancy specified
reasoning: Parent story: 'any live-trial conditions are issued and tracked out-of-band via Range Safety Case.' But approval decision track must be captured in Workbench audit log — loss of any panel action makes the decision non-defensible in accident inquiry. RPO zero (every action committed before UI acknowledgment). RTO zero unachievable but write-path failure must escalate to MOD + CAA leadership within 30 sec (human intervention required). Current architecture likely uses single-node relational DB — insufficient redundancy.
recommendation: Migrate audit/deliberation log to append-only distributed ledger (Kafka 3-node cluster, replication factor 3, min.insync.replicas=2). Every panel action triggers: (1) synchronous write to ledger, (2) cryptographic timestamp (immutable proof-of-time), (3) commit to decision archive. On write failure: immediately page on-call DBAs + MOD ITSO (escalation SLA < 1 min). Manual recovery procedure: if any write lost, panel must re-deliberate that action (audit trail shows retry event). Monthly log integrity audit: cryptographic hash of entire segment, verify against signed manifest.

title: Post-decision archive RPO must be zero but backup strategy not defined; regulatory retention at risk
reasoning: Regulatory retention (90+ year hold) is non-negotiable. Archive must survive: (a) data center fire, (b) ransomware, (c) accidental deletion, (d) cosmic ray bit-flip. Current 99.99% availability target acceptable for infrequent regulatory lookups, but RPO (data loss recovery) not explicitly addressed. 3-2-1 rule (3 copies, 2 media types, 1 off-site) not mentioned. Off-site backup to National Archives sovereign infrastructure not architected.
recommendation: Implement 3-2-1 backup: (3) copies — primary write-optimized node + two read-replica zones, (2) media — hot replicas + cold archive tape in UK facility, (1) off-site — encrypted backup to National Archives sovereign infrastructure. Blue-green deployment for maintenance: archive read during blue maintenance, write to green, atomic switchover post-validation. Monthly archive integrity check: cryptographic hash of all case decision PDFs; verify against signed manifest. Annual tape restore drill (audit trail demonstrates successful retrieval from National Archives backup).

title: Shared database backend between applicant workspace and panel tooling creates correlation failure risk (K1 style)
reasoning: If applicant workspace and panel tooling share primary DB backend, a single DB failure cascades: (1) applicants cannot submit, (2) panel cannot deliberate simultaneously. Per Effective Performance Engineering 'keystone' principle (K1): if geofence and autonomy share compute, they share failure. Same logic here: if audit log and panel action succeed but case metadata fails to write, audit shows decision but case is missing, resulting in non-defensible state. Correlation risk unmitigated.
recommendation: Decouple storage tiers: (1) applicant workspace DB — cost-optimized, 99.5% availability acceptable, async replication OK, (2) panel operations DB — high-availability (99.9%), synchronous replication required, (3) audit/deliberation ledger — append-only, separate from relational DB, 100% write guarantee, (4) archive DB — immutable snapshot after decision finalized. Each tier has independent failover strategy; no shared critical path. Enforce 'no shared single point of failure' rule: audit log writes must succeed even if case metadata fails (transaction commits atomically to ledger, then to archive, asynchronously to case DB).

title: Identity provider as external dependency SPOF; no local fallback for classified or unclassified access
reasoning: Both classified (MOD LDAP) and unclassified (CAA OAuth2) access depend on external identity providers. If MOD LDAP unreachable, no classified evidence can be accessed (panel cannot proceed). If CAA OAuth2 unreachable, applicants cannot log in (but offline pack submission possible). No mention of local authentication cache or fallback. REQ015 (CAA/MAA Interface Management) assumes identity is seamless; not true.
recommendation: Implement OAuth2/LDAP token caching with 24h TTL (cached identity valid for extended session even if provider unavailable). Fallback to time-limited session refresh using cached token + biometric re-verification (fingerprint on panel device). For classified access: implement local MOD LDAP replica (cached from primary, updated hourly, read-only). Test provider outage failover quarterly: block identity service, verify cached tokens still allow access, measure time-to-first-cache-miss. Emergency procedure: manual identity re-verification by CAA/MOD administrator if both primary + cache unavailable (audit log events marked 'offline mode').

title: DAA sensor availability during flight must be 99.99% but dual-redundancy path not specified in REQ007
reasoning: REQ007 specifies DAA evidence per DO-365 / ED-267 but does not mandate redundant sensors. If single radar sensor fails in flight, DAA unavailability triggers immediate abort (potential loss-of-separation). Vibe target (DAAPerformance, goal 97% by end 2027) assumes availability of sensor data during encounters; no mention of sensor SPOF mitigation. Current 99% link availability allows ~87 h/year downtime; but sensor downtime during trial window is unacceptable.
recommendation: Mandate dual independent DAA sensors per aircraft: radar + vision-based (or dual-frequency radar). Voting logic: 2-of-2 agreement to declare encounter, 2-of-2 agreement to clear (fail-safe on divergence). Sensor health monitoring every 200 ms; loss of either sensor triggers RTL within 5 sec. Pre-flight verification: apply known ADS-B stimuli (recorded replay) to both sensors, verify detection latency <200 ms for both. Test sensor redundancy monthly with controlled encounters (simulator or known traffic). Log all sensor detections + fusion decisions to independent flight data recorder.

title: Flight termination independent path (REQ011) lacks failover clarity; 99.99% availability unachievable if shared MCU
reasoning: REQ011 mandates 'independent termination' but does not specify hardware independence from primary autonomy MCU. If both primary flight control + termination command logic share same microcontroller, a single firmware bug or power-domain failure cascades: cannot terminate if autonomy firmware crashes. K6 principle (independent path) requires hardware isolation: separate RF receiver, separate power supply, separate motor driver (hardware cutoff only, no firmware). Current architecture likely violates this; RTO zero unachievable with shared MCU.
recommendation: Design independent hardware termination path: (1) dedicated RF receiver (separate crystal oscillator + demodulator), (2) independent power supply (super-capacitor or isolated battery), (3) hardware-only motor cutoff (no microcontroller involved, direct solid-state relay logic). Termination command must execute in <50 ms with zero software intervention. Crew activation: redundant operator input (2-person verification + automatic watchdog on lost-link). Pre-flight verification: apply termination command to one test motor (ground test), verify instant cutoff without delay. Log termination command + motor response to separate flight data recorder (independent from primary). Annual third-party certification audit (CAA-approved independent test lab).

title: C2 link resilience target (REQ006, Vibe 90 sec) implies autonomous swarm capability not yet proven; gap between wish (60 sec) and goal (90 sec) reflects immaturity
reasoning: Vibe targets show progression: wish 60 sec (end Phase 3, current status 45 sec), goal 90 sec (end Phase 5, stretch feasibility), stretch 300 sec with combined C2+GPS loss (uncertain feasibility). Current C2 link resilience (45 sec) insufficient for BVLOS swarm with autonomous decision-making. Goal (90 sec) requires mature swarm coordination algorithms; not yet demonstrated. If goal not achieved by end Phase 5, trial must continue with crew takeover as failsafe (limits autonomy benefit).
recommendation: Implement tiered C2 loss response: (1) 0-5 sec loss: autonomous hold formation, reduce throttle to slow descent, (2) 5-30 sec loss: autonomous RTL to last-known safe zone (geofence center), (3) 30-90 sec loss: crew takeover window (if C2 restored, crew resumes control; if not, RTL continues), (4) >90 sec: flight termination (if no other option). Dual-path C2: licensed UHF primary + satellite fallback (or dual-frequency ground stations). Test autonomous survival monthly: controlled RF blackout, verify formation hold <200 ms response time, RTL initiated correctly. Document which behaviors remain autonomous vs crew-dependent; use to set realistic SAIL/OSO envelopes in SORA.

title: Ground control segment availability during trial window not explicitly required; but unspecified failover creates trial abort risk
reasoning: Parent user story: Workbench unavailability shall not block trial (out-of-band via Range Safety Case). But ground control station failure = trial abort, potential safety risk if swarm enters off-nominal state during abort. Current architecture likely single control station per trial; dual-operator crew provides human redundancy but not system redundancy. Network path to airfield (ISP/fiber) is SPOF. Pre-flight ground-station validation not mandated.
recommendation: Deploy dual ground control stations (primary + hot-standby) at trial location with identical crew briefing and switchover procedure. Network redundancy: dual ISP paths (different providers/technologies) or satellite backup. Automated failover: if primary station loses telemetry >500 ms, alert standby crew (pre-briefed on last-known swarm state). Telemetry logging to redundant independent recorders. Test ground-station failover monthly with live crew; post-test debrief documents switchover time + crew workload. Pre-trial validation: 30 min ground-test with both stations active, verify both receive same telemetry stream.
